overview: Eric Ries argues that short-termism is not a law of nature but a flaw in human-made financial infrastructure — and infrastructure can be rebuilt. The Long-Term Stock Exchange, SEC-approved as only the fifth national securities exchange, operationalizes that belief: companies make binding, enforceable pledges to long-term principles, long-tenured shareholders can earn superior voting rights or dividends, and the exchange earns money by serving companies rather than traders.

The arc traces how Ries repeatedly backs polarizing ideas that later become consensus — a test reader begged him to cut the exchange idea from The Lean Startup, just as VCs once yelled at him for describing continuous deployment. The unresolved tension is whether LTSE can survive entrenched incumbents who profit from the status quo and persuade any company to list first, when maybe everybody would like to go second.

takeaways.title: Broken LTSE pledges become securities violations
takeaways.detail: Listed companies must register binding operational commitments for each long-term principle, and violating one carries real enforcement penalties. This makes the standards self-selecting: only companies genuinely serious about long-termism would rationally sign up, unlike aspirational S-1 language investors ignore.

takeaways.title: Long-term holders can earn superior voting rights
takeaways.detail: Ries's middle ground on dual-class stock — which he compares to feudalism when perpetual — lets investors earn into the privileged class through holding tenure, via vote multipliers or progressive dividends. LTSE software tracks long-term ownership in real time, so rewards adjust automatically when holders trade out.

takeaways.title: Algorithmic and passive trading drained price information
takeaways.detail: Most trades are now machines hitting buttons and much ownership sits in index funds, so far fewer active human voices set prices and a single activist can drag passive money with them. One quant with a ten-minute average holding period still had to vote proxies — governance assigned to someone who says shorting cattle futures doesn't bother the cows.

takeaways.title: Staying private longer follows from policy choices
takeaways.detail: Ries reframes a VC's case for staying private — unlimited capital, any terms, no disclosure — as a bit field of switches that flip when conditions change, citing a thermostatic equilibrium between private and public markets. WeWork proved the point: its last-gasp rescue attempt was an IPO, and undisclosed secondary transactions echo the fraud his grandparents' Depression-era rules were built to stop.

takeaways.title: LTSE sells software to companies, not trades
takeaways.detail: Instead of per-trade take rates that reward volume, LTSE monetizes tools like captable.io, hiringplan.io, and startuprunway.io from seed through IPO, plus services such as placing large blocks with long-term investors after market close. This inverts the incumbent model where investors are the customer and companies are merely 'issuers.'

chapters.title: The page a test reader begged him to cut
chapters.summary: The LTSE began as the final idea in The Lean Startup manuscript, and a test reader warned it would destroy the book's credibility. Ries treats that hostility as validation, setting the episode's theme that his polarizing ideas precede consensus.
chapters.quote: you basically piss away the credibility you've carefully built up

chapters.title: There.com's waterfall launch to absent customers
chapters.summary: The virtual-world startup raised $50 million, hired nearly 200 people, and staffed a warehouse of support reps before launch — the formative failure that later crystallized into Lean Startup vocabulary.
chapters.quote: the customers never read the business plan

chapters.title: Steve Blank's customer development class at Berkeley
chapters.summary: Blank invested in IMVU on the condition that the founders audit his class, where MBAs argued his ideas were nonsense. This is the intellectual lineage of Lean Startup: marketing-side rigor meeting Ries's engineering practice.
chapters.quote: you need to aggressively listen to your customers

chapters.title: Short-termism treated as a law of gravity
chapters.summary: Ries's decade of consulting revealed that every organization complains about quarter-to-quarter thinking yet treats capital markets as unchangeable facts of nature. Recognizing them as human creations is the founding insight of LTSE.
chapters.quote: It's like asking, what are we going to do about gravity?

chapters.title: Binding pledges instead of S-1 platitudes
chapters.summary: The core mechanism distinguishing LTSE: companies operationalize each principle in an enforceable registered commitment, with the exchange as certifying body. Flexibility in implementation avoids one-size-fits-all while keeping the pledge real.
chapters.quote: every company has to do something real

chapters.title: Machines, index funds, and vanishing market voices
chapters.summary: David maps the structural trends motivating LTSE — algorithmic trading, passive index ownership, and abstraction on abstraction — which mute information flow and let short-term arrivals swing control of companies.
chapters.quote: It's machines hitting buttons and they hit them a lot faster

chapters.title: A middle path between feudalism and one-share-one-vote
chapters.summary: Rather than perpetual founder control that a successor CEO can ignore, Ries proposes letting long-term investors earn superior rights over time — solving the succession flaw in dual-class structures.
chapters.quote: They have to be able to earn their way into the better class

chapters.title: The quant who shorts cattle futures
chapters.summary: Ries's story of a trader baffled that his artificial short sent 3,000 middle managers scrambling shows short-term traders are amoral, not immoral — and would happily hand governance to someone else.
chapters.quote: When I short cattle futures, the cows don't care

chapters.title: The bit field keeping companies private
chapters.summary: Ries dissects a VC's argument that private markets make IPOs pointless, predicting the switches would flip — and WeWork's failed IPO-as-rescue, plus opaque secondary sales, suggest the reckoning has begun.
chapters.quote: why don't you call me back when the bleep hits the fan

chapters.title: A stock exchange where companies are the customer
chapters.summary: LTSE inverts the exchange business model: revenue comes from software and services sold to companies and long-term investors — including block placements after market close — rather than from trading volume.
chapters.quote: the only stock exchange you will ever meet where you're the customer

chapters.title: Grading the A-plus case: value created, maybe not captured
chapters.summary: Ries concedes LTSE could fix a trillion-dollar problem in capitalism while capturing little of the value, and says he warns IRR-driven investors away — the mission is the return.
chapters.quote: create that order of magnitude of value or die trying

chapters.title: The failure modes: entrenched enemies and the go-first problem
chapters.summary: Beyond regulators and sabotage by incumbents profiting from the status quo, the existential risk is that every company waits for another to list first — a risk concierge MVPs and letters of intent can only partially retire.
chapters.quote: Maybe everybody would like to go second
